title: A US military exercise in space got underway with barely anyone noticing
gist: Rocket Lab quietly launched a Space Force satellite (Victus Haze Puma) from New Zealand to rendezvous with and inspect a True Anomaly target satellite, demonstrating the US military's ability to rapidly respond to on-orbit threats; total mission cost ~$92M.

insights.claim: Rocket Lab launched the Space Force's Victus Haze Puma satellite from New Zealand and within eight hours it approached within 60 miles of True Anomaly's Jackal-0004 target satellite, demonstrating rapid rendezvous-and-proximity-operations to assess an adversary-simulated threat.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Demonstrates an operational US capability for on-orbit inspection/interception; tailwind for Rocket Lab and True Anomaly defense revenue.

insights.claim: Victus Haze cost about $92 million funded by a mix of government funding and private capital, building on the 2023 Victus Nox mission where Firefly launched a satellite just 27 hours after receiving Space Force orders.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Quantifies the cost of responsive-space demos and the public-private funding model; benchmarks for defense space budgeting.
insights.locator: Victus Haze is a more complicated demo

insights.claim: The mission was conducted with near-total secrecy — no advance announcement, no livestream, and no official acknowledgment from Rocket Lab or the Space Force — with the launch only confirmed via the military's space-object catalog.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Operational secrecy is itself part of the responsive-space doctrine; signals national-security missions may go undisclosed.

insights.claim: True Anomaly's Victus Haze contribution was originally booked on Firefly's Alpha rocket but moved to SpaceX's Falcon 9 after an Alpha launch failure grounded the rocket for nearly a year.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Single-vehicle dependency is a program risk; Falcon 9 remains the fallback when newer launchers stumble.
insights.locator: Victus Haze is a more complicated demo

insights.claim: True Anomaly validated its Jackal satellite's closed-loop tracking of maneuvering targets during simultaneous vehicle maneuvers, confirming end-to-end uncooperative rendezvous and proximity operations capability.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Technical milestone establishing True Anomaly's autonomous tracking software as mission-ready for national-security work.
insights.locator: The tests of the Jackal satellite platform
